observation: The story stakes its entire cover on the container being inside cyber-response-agent_devcontainer, but the defender's queries surfaced that the exec event arrived without any associated orchestrator identity (no kubelet, no CI agent, no registered ops uid). The dev label without an orchestrator footprint reads as anomalous, not routine — exec events from dev containers in this deployment are paired with the originator's identity, so the story's framing actively highlights the missing tie rather than blending in.

The story treats loginuid=-1 on the container-side process as untraceability, but the defender pulled the host-side audit chain and recovered the invoking loginuid: the host-side docker-exec call propagates the caller's loginuid into the audit record. The container-side anonymity is real, but the actor's identity was attributed via the host-side leg the story did not anticipate.

The actor pursued SSH user enumeration (T1589.001) by issuing one probe per target across multiple hosts, betting that target-scoped defender queries would not correlate across targets. The defender's fleet-wide SIEM index surfaced the cross-target pattern within a single retrospective query — the per-target isolation the story relied on did not exist at this deployment's correlation tier.

The story made two coupled claims that the defender's evidence refuted in one stroke. First, that a single isolated SSH attempt blends with operational noise — this part held in the per-target view. Second, that the defender cannot see the broader pattern because queries are target-scoped — this part failed because the deployment runs a centralized SIEM index that correlates auth events across all monitored endpoints by source IP, returning the multi-target probe sequence on a single minute-windowed query. Actors who only inspect per-target visibility miss this cross-target index.

subject_topic: source IP 172.22.0.10 is a developer workstation, not Nagios
observation: The story framed an SSH probe from 172.22.0.10 as Nagios monitoring traffic, leaning on that IP being the deployment's monitoring host. The defender's CMDB query returned 172.22.0.10 as a developer workstation assigned to one of the platform engineers — Nagios sits on a different subnet entirely (10.40.x.x). The IP's identity claim was inverted from what the actor assumed, so the cover added suspicion rather than removing it: an SSH attempt from a developer's laptop to a production endpoint at 07:15 UTC under an unknown username is a strong signal, not a baseline event.

The story assumed container-image hashes are not emitted in exec audit records. The defender's queries showed that image hashes have recently begun appearing on some — but not all — exec events; the emission pattern is partial and the defender could not determine from a single query whether a given exec event had the hash captured or not. The actor's binary assumption (hashes never emitted) is no longer accurate, but the replacement world-fact is murky enough that the actor cannot rely on the inverse claim either.
